content:
# Joshua Chapter 10 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 233-235 | Codex Vaticanus (LXX)
**Source Image:** `JOS_010_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_010_page_002.jpg`, `JOS_010_page_003.jpg`

1. And it was, when Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem heard that Joshua had captured Ai and had devoted it to destruction — as he had done to Jericho and to its king, so he had done to Ai and to its king — and that the inhabitants of Gibeon had made peace with Israel and were in their midst,

2. then they feared greatly, because Gibeon was a great city, like one of the royal cities, and because it was greater than Ai, and all its men were mighty.

3. And Adoni-Zedek king of Jerusalem sent to Hoham king of Hebron, and to Piram king of Jarmuth, and to Japhia king of Lachish, and to Debir king of Eglon, saying,

4. "Come up to me and help me, and let us strike Gibeon, for it has made peace with Joshua and with the sons of Israel."

5. And the five kings of the Amorites gathered and went up — the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, the king of Eglon — they and all their camps, and they encamped against Gibeon and made war against it.

6. And the men of Gibeon sent to Joshua, to the camp at Gilgal, saying, "Do not let your hands drop from your servants. Come up to us quickly and save us and help us, for all the kings of the Amorites, the inhabitants of the hill country, have gathered against us."

7. And Joshua went up from Gilgal, he and all the people of war with him, and all the mighty men of valor.

8. And YHWH said to Joshua, "Do not fear them, for into your hand I have given them. Not a man of them shall stand before you."

9. And Joshua came upon them suddenly — all the night he went up from Gilgal.

10. And YHWH threw them into confusion before Israel, and he struck them with a great striking at Gibeon, and he pursued them by the way of the ascent of Beth-Horon, and he struck them as far as Azekah and as far as Makkedah.

11. And it was, as they fled before Israel — they were on the descent of Beth-Horon — and YHWH cast great stones upon them from the heavens, as far as Azekah, and they died. More were those who died by the stones of the hail than those whom the sons of Israel killed with the sword.

12. Then Joshua spoke to YHWH on the day YHWH gave the Amorites before the sons of Israel, and he said in the sight of Israel,

"Sun, be still over Gibeon,
and moon, in the valley of Aijalon."

13. And the sun was still, and the moon stood, until the nation took vengeance on its enemies.

Is it not written in the Book of the Upright? And the sun stood in the midst of the heavens, and did not hasten to go down for about a whole day.

14. And there was no day like that, before it or after it, when YHWH listened to the voice of a man, for YHWH was fighting for Israel.

15. And Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.

16. And these five kings fled and hid themselves in the cave at Makkedah.

17. And it was told to Joshua, saying, "The five kings have been found, hidden in the cave at Makkedah."

18. And Joshua said, "Roll great stones to the mouth of the cave, and set men over it to guard them.

19. And as for you, do not stand still. Pursue your enemies and strike their rear. Do not let them enter their cities, for YHWH your God has given them into your hand."

20. And it was, when Joshua and the sons of Israel had finished striking them with a very great striking until they were consumed — and the survivors who survived of them entered the fortified cities —

21. then all the people returned to the camp, to Joshua at Makkedah, in peace. No man sharpened his tongue against the sons of Israel.

22. And Joshua said, "Open the mouth of the cave, and bring out to me these five kings from the cave."

23. And they did so, and they brought out to him the five kings from the cave: the king of Jerusalem, the king of Hebron, the king of Jarmuth, the king of Lachish, the king of Eglon.

24. And it was, when they brought out these kings to Joshua, that Joshua called to every man of Israel and said to the commanders of the men of war who had gone with him, "Draw near, put your feet on the necks of these kings." And they drew near and put their feet on their necks.

25. And Joshua said to them, "Do not fear and do not be dismayed. Be strong and be courageous, for thus YHWH will do to all your enemies against whom you fight."

26. And Joshua struck them after this and put them to death, and he hung them on five trees. And they were hanging on the trees until the evening.

27. And it was, at the time of the going down of the sun, that Joshua commanded, and they took them down from the trees and cast them into the cave where they had hidden themselves, and they set great stones against the mouth of the cave — unto this very day.

28. And Joshua captured Makkedah on that day and struck it with the edge of the sword, and its king he devoted to destruction — them and every soul that was in it. He left no survivor. And he did to the king of Makkedah as he had done to the king of Jericho.

29. And Joshua passed on, and all Israel with him, from Makkedah to Libnah, and he made war against Libnah.

30. And YHWH gave it also, and its king, into the hand of Israel, and he struck it with the edge of the sword, and every soul that was in it. He left no survivor in it. And he did to its king as he had done to the king of Jericho.

31. And Joshua passed on, and all Israel with him, from Libnah to Lachish, and he encamped against it and made war against it.

32. And YHWH gave Lachish into the hand of Israel, and he captured it on the second day, and he struck it with the edge of the sword, and every soul that was in it, according to all that he had done to Libnah.

33. Then Horam king of Gezer went up to help Lachish, and Joshua struck him and his people until he had left him no survivor.

34. And Joshua passed on, and all Israel with him, from Lachish to Eglon, and they encamped against it and made war against it.

35. And they captured it on that day and struck it with the edge of the sword, and every soul that was in it he devoted to destruction on that day, according to all that he had done to Lachish.

36. And Joshua went up, and all Israel with him, from Eglon to Hebron, and they made war against it.

37. And they captured it and struck it with the edge of the sword, and its king and all its cities and every soul that was in it. He left no survivor, according to all that he had done to Eglon. And he devoted it to destruction, and every soul that was in it.

38. And Joshua turned back, and all Israel with him, to Debir, and he made war against it.

39. And he captured it, and its king, and all its cities, and they struck them with the edge of the sword, and they devoted to destruction every soul that was in it. He left no survivor. As he had done to Hebron, so he did to Debir and to its king, and as he had done to Libnah and to its king.

40. And Joshua struck all the land: the hill country and the Negev and the lowland and the slopes, and all their kings. He left no survivor, and all that breathed he devoted to destruction, as YHWH the God of Israel had commanded.

41. And Joshua struck them from Kadesh-Barnea as far as Gaza, and all the land of Goshen as far as Gibeon.

42. And all these kings and their land Joshua captured at one time, for YHWH the God of Israel was fighting for Israel.

43. And Joshua returned, and all Israel with him, to the camp at Gilgal.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Joshua 10 is one of the most extraordinary chapters in the Hebrew Bible — a battle narrative that escalates to cosmic dimensions, with YHWH fighting through divine panic, hailstones from heaven, and the suspension of the sun and moon. The chapter then transitions into a systematic southern campaign account that follows a rigid formulaic structure. All three translators handled this material with care and substantial agreement.

### Areas of Broad Consensus

- All three translators correctly render YHWH throughout (thirteen occurrences: vv. 8, 10, 11, 12 [twice], 14 [twice], 19, 25, 30, 32, 40, 42), with the full title "YHWH the God of Israel" in vv. 40 and 42.
- All three translators agreed on the transliteration of "Adoni-Zedek" and recognized its meaning ("my lord is righteousness").
- All three preserved the distinction between the divine imperative "be still" (dom) and the narrative report "stood" (amad) in the sun-standing episode (vv. 12-13).
- All three identified the "Book of the Upright" (Sefer HaYashar) as a lost Israelite poetic source, though PNEUMA transliterated it as "Jashar" (see Disagreement Log).
- All three preserved the formulaic repetition of the southern campaign (vv. 28-39) without variation, recognizing the theological and literary weight of the deliberate repetition.
- All three noted the significant LXX Vaticanus omission of verse 15, which may represent a later harmonizing addition in the MT tradition (anticipation of v. 43). SOPHIA follows the MT as primary text and retains the verse.
- All three rendered the "edge of the sword" idiom (literally "mouth of the sword") consistently through the campaign formula.
- All three preserved the "no survivor" (lo hish'ir sarid) formula throughout the southern campaign.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **Verse 2 — "they feared greatly":** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in preserving the plural verb, which is the reading of the MT (vayyir'u me'od). PNEUMA's impersonal rendering ("the fear was enormous") captures atmosphere but departs from the Hebrew grammatical subject. The plural likely includes Adoni-Zedek and his allies or populace.

2. **Verse 6 — "Do not let your hands drop from your servants":** SOPHIA preserves the Hebrew idiom (al tereph yadekha), following EMET's literal approach and LOGOS's formal rendering, rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "Do not abandon your servants." The idiom is vivid and comprehensible in English.

3. **Verse 10 — "threw them into confusion":** SOPHIA adopts this rendering (LOGOS), which preserves the divine agency of the verb wayhummem while remaining natural English. EMET's "confused-them" is too compressed; PNEUMA's "threw them into panic" slightly shifts the semantic range (confusion vs. panic). "Confusion" better represents the root h-m-m as attested in Exodus 14:24.

4. **Verse 11 — Hailstone sequence:** The MT presents "great stones from the heavens" followed by the interpretive gloss "stones of the hail." SOPHIA preserves this two-stage revelation: "great stones...from the heavens" and then "stones of the hail," following EMET and LOGOS rather than PNEUMA's compressed "enormous hailstones."

5. **Verses 12-13 — The sun poem:** SOPHIA renders this as set-off poetry to mark the register shift from prose to the quotation from the Book of the Upright. The imperative dom is rendered "be still" (EMET, LOGOS) rather than "stand still" (PNEUMA), since the Hebrew root d-m-m means "be silent, cease" rather than "stand." The LXX's steto ("let it stand") interprets rather than translates.

6. **Verse 13 — "did not hasten to go down":** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in preserving the anthropomorphism of the sun "not hastening" — a deliberate personification in the Hebrew (lo 'ats lavo'). PNEUMA's "was in no hurry to set" captures the same sense with more idiomatic English but is slightly too free for a consensus text.

7. **Verse 13 — "Book of the Upright":** SOPHIA translates yashar as "Upright" rather than transliterating as "Jashar" (PNEUMA), following EMET and LOGOS. The word has a clear Hebrew meaning ("upright, straight") that should be conveyed.

8. **Verse 15 — Inclusion:** Despite the LXX Vaticanus omission, SOPHIA retains verse 15 following the MT (Leningrad Codex), which all three translators included. The verse may indeed be a scribal anticipation of verse 43, but the MT reading is primary.

9. **Verse 19 — "strike their rear":** SOPHIA adopts a middle rendering between EMET's hyper-literal "strike-their-rear" and PNEUMA's dynamic "cut down their rear guard." LOGOS's "attack their rear" is close; SOPHIA uses "strike" for consistency with the chapter's dominant verb (nakah).

10. **Verse 21 — "No man sharpened his tongue":** SOPHIA preserves this vivid Hebrew idiom (lo charats...leshono), following EMET and LOGOS, rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "No one dared to say a word against." The idiom (cf. Exodus 11:7) is arresting and comprehensible.

11. **Verse 27 — "unto this very day":** All three translators converge on rendering 'ad-'etsem hayom hazeh with the intensifying "very," marking the narrator's temporal vantage point. SOPHIA follows the consensus.

12. **Verses 28-39 — Campaign formula:** SOPHIA preserves the rigid repetitive structure without stylistic variation, following the unanimous instinct of all three translators. The drumbeat repetition — pass on, encamp, capture, strike with the edge of the sword, every soul, no survivor, devoted to destruction — is a deliberate literary device carrying theological weight.

13. **Verse 40 — "all that breathed":** SOPHIA renders kol hanneshamah as "all that breathed" following LOGOS, which conveys the Hebrew neshamah ("breath of life," cf. Genesis 2:7) more naturally than EMET's "all the-breathing" and more literally than PNEUMA's "everything that breathed."

14. **Verse 42 — "at one time":** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in rendering b'pa'am 'achath as "at one time" rather than PNEUMA's interpretive "in a single campaign." The Hebrew denotes temporal compression, not necessarily a single campaign, and "at one time" is closer to the idiomatic range.

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex pages 233-235 show the text of Joshua 10 in the standard three-column format with Masoretic annotations. The text is clearly legible across all three folios, with the chapter spanning from the end of Joshua 9 on page 233 through to the beginning of Joshua 11 on page 235. The Vaticanus LXX folio (Vat.gr.1209) confirms the Greek parallel in continuous uncial script, with the notable absence of verse 15 consistent with the critical apparatus.

---
## Disagreement Log

See `/disagreements/JOS_010.md` for the full divergence record.

### Summary of Divergences

Divergences were identified in **twenty-three** of the forty-three verses (vv. 1, 2, 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 19, 20, 21, 24, 25, 26, 27, 28, 30, 33, 37, 40, 42). The remaining twenty verses (vv. 3, 4, 8, 9, 15, 16, 17, 18, 22, 23, 29, 31, 32, 34, 35, 36, 38, 39, 41, 43) achieved near-total agreement. The most significant divergences concerned: the rendering of dom ("be still" vs. "stand still") in v. 12, the "Book of the Upright" vs. "Book of Jashar" in v. 13, the hailstone description sequence in v. 11, the idiom in v. 6 ("hands drop" vs. "abandon"), the tongue-sharpening idiom in v. 21, the cherem vocabulary ("devoted to destruction" vs. "put under the ban"), and the temporal phrase in v. 42 ("at one time" vs. "in a single campaign").
